verses.text: There was a certain man of Ramathaim-zophim, of mount Ephraim, and his name was Elkanah, the son of Jeroham, the son of Elihu, the son of Tohu, the son of Zuph, an Ephrathite: and he had two wives; the name of the one was Hannah, and the name of the other Peninnah: and Peninnah had children, but Hannah had no children. Guidance: A Levite-descended family (1 Chron 6:33-38 traces Elkanah to Levi) living in Ephraim's hill country. Polygamy in scripture is described, never commended; the same chapter that records it also exposes its cost. 'Hannah' means 'grace'; 'Peninnah' means 'pearl' or 'coral.' Hannah is named first, even though Peninnah has the children — a quiet signal that the narrator's heart is with the barren one. The book of judges has just closed; the book of kings is about to open; in between, a private domestic story in a family no one outside Ramah has heard of. The LORD's biggest histories often start at the smallest hearths.

There was a certain man of Ramathaim-zophim, of mount Ephraim, and his name was Elkanah, the son of Jeroham, the son of Elihu, the son of Tohu, the son of Zuph, an Ephrathite: and he had two wives; the name of the one was Hannah, and the name of the other Peninnah: and Peninnah had children, but Hannah had no children. Guidance: A Levite-descended family (1 Chron 6:33-38 traces Elkanah to Levi) living in Ephraim's hill country. Polygamy in scripture is described, never commended; the same chapter that records it also exposes its cost. 'Hannah' means 'grace'; 'Peninnah' means 'pearl' or 'coral.' Hannah is named first, even though Peninnah has the children — a quiet signal that the narrator's heart is with the barren one. The book of judges has just closed; the book of kings is about to open; in between, a private domestic story in a family no one outside Ramah has heard of. The LORD's biggest histories often start at the smallest hearths.

This man went up out of his city yearly to worship and to sacrifice unto the LORD of hosts in Shiloh. The two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, the priests of the LORD, were there. When the time was that Elkanah offered, he gave to Peninnah his wife, and to all her sons and her daughters, portions: but unto Hannah he gave a worthy portion; for he loved Hannah: but the LORD had shut up her womb. Guidance: Yearly pilgrimage to Shiloh, where the tabernacle had been for three centuries (Josh 18:1). Notice the priests named: Hophni and Phinehas — chapter 2 will reveal them as corrupt. The tabernacle is running on bad clergy. Elkanah loves Hannah and shows it — a 'worthy portion' (Heb: a double or honor portion) of meat at the sacrificial meal. But the same verse drops the divine attribution: 'the LORD had shut up her womb.' Not bad luck, not Hannah's fault — God Himself has closed it. The text wants the reader to feel both the husband's love and the LORD's sovereignty in the same sorrow.

Her adversary also provoked her sore, for to make her fret, because the LORD had shut up her womb. As he did so year by year, when she went up to the house of the LORD, so she provoked her; therefore she wept, and did not eat. Then said Elkanah her husband to her, Hannah, why weepest thou? and why eatest thou not? and why is thy heart grieved? am not I better to thee than ten sons? Guidance: 'Her adversary' — the narrator names Peninnah honestly. Year after year, at the very moment Hannah is trying to worship, the other wife strikes the wound. Religious gatherings can host cruelty that ordinary days hide. Hannah's response is to refuse food and to weep. Elkanah, well-meaning, asks four questions that miss her depth. 'Am not I better to thee than ten sons?' The kindest sentence a husband can say; the wrongest answer to grief. He thinks she wants a child to compete with Peninnah. She wants a child because she has been told by her people that motherhood is the meaning of a Hebrew woman. Comforters often answer questions they were not asked.

So Hannah rose up after they had eaten in Shiloh, and after they had drunk. Now Eli the priest sat upon a seat by a post of the temple of the LORD. And she was in bitterness of soul, and prayed unto the LORD, and wept sore. And she vowed a vow, and said, O LORD of hosts, if thou wilt indeed look on the affliction of thine handmaid, and remember me, and not forget thine handmaid, but wilt give unto thine handmaid a man child, then I will give him unto the LORD all the days of his life, and there shall no razor come upon his head. Guidance: Hannah stops weeping and starts praying. She walks past the meal into the LORD's house. Her prayer is structured like a covenant petition — three parallel clauses: 'look on the affliction… remember me… not forget thine handmaid.' Then the vow: a Nazirite-from-the-womb (same vow Samson's mother received unilaterally; Hannah offers it back voluntarily). She does not ask for a son to keep; she asks for a son to give. The child she lacks she will dedicate before he is conceived. Some prayers move God by their willingness to surrender what they are still asking for.

It came to pass, as she continued praying before the LORD, that Eli marked her mouth. Now Hannah, she spake in her heart; only her lips moved, but her voice was not heard: therefore Eli thought she had been drunken. And Eli said unto her, How long wilt thou be drunken? put away thy wine from thee. Guidance: Hannah prays silently — unusual for that era when prayers were typically spoken aloud. Eli, the high priest, watches her mouth move without sound and concludes she is drunk. The narrator's irony is sharp: the most spiritual moment in Shiloh is being witnessed by the priesthood and misread. Eli is so accustomed to his sons' drunkenness around the sanctuary that he assumes the same of every worshiper. Religious leaders trained by bad examples will misjudge the genuine when it finally appears. Eli's first rebuke in scripture is wrong.

Hannah answered and said, No, my lord, I am a woman of a sorrowful spirit: I have drunk neither wine nor strong drink, but have poured out my soul before the LORD. Count not thine handmaid for a daughter of Belial: for out of the abundance of my complaint and grief have I spoken hitherto. Guidance: Hannah corrects him gently. 'A woman of sorrowful spirit' — she names her state honestly. 'Poured out my soul' — the verb of full self-disclosure to God. 'A daughter of Belial' is the phrase for utter wickedness (cf. Judges 19); she is asking Eli not to file her under that category. She does not match his rudeness; she explains her depth. Sometimes the right response to being misread by a leader is not anger but a calm window into your heart. Eli, to his credit, will believe her.

Then Eli answered and said, Go in peace: and the God of Israel grant thee thy petition that thou hast asked of him. She said, Let thine handmaid find grace in thy sight. So the woman went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad. Guidance: Eli pronounces a benediction. He does not promise the LORD will say yes; he asks the LORD to grant. But it is enough. Hannah goes back to eat — the meal she had been refusing — and 'her countenance was no more sad.' Her circumstances have not changed; her child has not been promised; only her heart has. Real prayer leaves the soul lighter even before the answer arrives. Hannah's faith is in the LORD's character, not in a confirmed outcome.

They rose up in the morning early, and worshipped before the LORD, and returned, and came to their house to Ramah: and Elkanah knew Hannah his wife; and the LORD remembered her. It came to pass, when the time was come about after Hannah had conceived, that she bare a son, and called his name Samuel, saying, Because I have asked him of the LORD. Guidance: 'The LORD remembered her.' Three words; a hinge. The verb 'remember' here is what God did for Noah after the flood, for Israel in Egypt, for Rachel before Joseph. It is not retrieval after forgetting; it is intervention after holding. She names the child 'Samuel' — sounds like 'asked of God' or 'heard of God.' His name is her testimony. Every time anyone calls his name for the next ninety years, they will be saying 'asked of God.' Some children are walking prayer-answers.

It is not retrieval after forgetting; it is intervention after holding. She names the child 'Samuel' — sounds like 'asked of God' or 'heard of God.' His name is her testimony. The man Elkanah, and all his house, went up to offer unto the LORD the yearly sacrifice, and his vow. But Hannah went not up; for she said unto her husband, I will not go up until the child be weaned, and then I will bring him, that he may appear before the LORD, and there abide for ever. Elkanah her husband said unto her, Do what seemeth thee good; tarry until thou have weaned him; only the LORD establish his word. So the woman abode, and gave her son suck until she weaned him. Guidance: Weaning in the ancient world was about three years. Hannah will keep her baby that long before fulfilling her vow. Elkanah supports her — 'do what seemeth thee good' — even though he could have legally annulled her vow (Num 30). The phrase 'only the LORD establish his word' assumes God spoke a promise; perhaps Hannah told her husband about Eli's benediction. The household is now praying together over the child Hannah promised away. Faithful spouses do not undo one another's vows; they help keep them.

When she had weaned him, she took him up with her, with three bullocks, and one ephah of flour, and a bottle of wine, and brought him unto the house of the LORD in Shiloh: and the child was young. They slew a bullock, and brought the child to Eli. Guidance: Three bullocks (one for the burnt offering, perhaps one for a fellowship offering, one for an additional offering), about twenty quarts of flour, and a skin of wine — a generous offering. Hannah arrives not empty-handed; she brings the most extravagant gift and the smallest hand. 'The child was young' — about three years old. The narrator tells us his age in passing to make sure we feel what Hannah is doing. She is leaving her tiny son in a household where the priest's own sons are corrupt.

She said, Oh my lord, as thy soul liveth, my lord, I am the woman that stood by thee here, praying unto the LORD. For this child I prayed; and the LORD hath given me my petition which I asked of him: therefore also I have lent him to the LORD; as long as he liveth he shall be lent to the LORD. And he worshipped the LORD there. Guidance: Hannah's introduction to Eli is layered. She reminds him of the praying woman he once mistook for drunk; she gently establishes that prayer was answered; then she gives the gift. 'I have lent him to the LORD' — the Hebrew verb (sha'al) is the same root as 'asked.' She asked God for him; she now gives back what she asked for. The same verb that names the child also names the surrender. 'As long as he liveth' — for life. The chapter's last verb is striking: 'he worshipped the LORD there.' Whether 'he' refers to little Samuel or to Elkanah, the chapter's last action is worship. Hannah brought a child; the LORD got worship; the nation will get a prophet. From one woman's silent prayer, the era of kings begins.
